stories.title: Uh-Oh! Facebook and Instagram Went Down for a While
stories.headline_kid: Facebook and Instagram stopped working properly for a bit today, and hundreds of people in Singapore couldn't open their apps!
summary: This afternoon, many people around the world — including here in Singapore — tried to open Facebook or Instagram and got error messages instead of their usual feed. Around 5pm, an outage tracker called Downdetector recorded about 430 reports from Instagram users in Singapore alone.

These apps are owned by a company called Meta. When something goes wrong with their computer systems, called servers, millions of people everywhere can be affected at the same time — a bit like when the whole class's Wi-Fi suddenly drops during a video call.
Meta has not yet explained exactly what caused the problem. Usually, these outages happen because of a technical glitch — a small but annoying fault in the system — rather than anything more serious.

The good news is that services like this are usually fixed within a few hours. But the outage was a reminder of just how much we depend on these apps every single day, from chatting with friends to running online businesses.

lenses.me: If you use Instagram to see your friends' posts or send messages, you might have found it wouldn't load for a while today.
lenses.family: Parents who sell things online through Facebook or Instagram shops might have worried about losing customers during the outage.
lenses.friends: Your friends were probably messaging each other, complaining that the apps 'cannot open leh!'
lenses.nation: It shows how much Singapore, like the rest of the world, relies on just a few big tech companies for everyday communication.
lenses.world: It's a reminder that when one company's system has a problem, it can affect people on almost every continent at once.

compo_words.meaning: a period when something, like electricity or an app, stops working
compo_words.example: The sudden outage left thousands of users unable to check their messages.

compo_words.meaning: a small, temporary problem in a computer system
compo_words.example: A tiny glitch in the app caused it to crash for everyone.

compo_words.meaning: depending heavily on something
compo_words.example: Many families are reliant on social media apps to stay in touch.

compo_words.meaning: to bring something back to its normal working state
compo_words.example: Engineers worked quickly to restore the app within hours.

compo_phrases.example: The app went down suddenly, leaving users confused.
By evening, Instagram was back up and running for most users.

questions: Have you ever been affected when an app you use suddenly stopped working? How did you feel?
Do you think people today rely too much on phone apps to communicate? Why or why not?
What would you do if you couldn't use your favourite app for a whole day?
phrases: I remember when...
In my opinion...
This made me feel...
If this happened to me, I would...
I think this shows that...

sources.name: CNA - Users of Meta's Facebook, Instagram report suffering some outages

stories.title: Singapore Politician Kenneth Jeyaretnam Passes Away
stories.headline_kid: Kenneth Jeyaretnam, who led the Reform Party, a Singapore political party, has passed away at the age of 67.
summary: Kenneth Jeyaretnam was the leader of Singapore's Reform Party, one of the political parties that take part in our country's elections. He was the son of J.B. Jeyaretnam, a well-known lawyer who was also a pioneering opposition politician in Singapore many years ago.

Sadly, Mr Jeyaretnam fell seriously ill while he was overseas in the United Kingdom. He later developed something called sepsis, which is when the body's fight against an infection becomes so strong that it starts to harm the body itself. Because he was so unwell, he was unable to return home to Singapore.

His wife shared that despite the family's efforts, he passed away after his final weeks in hospital. He had spent many years speaking up about how Singapore's economy and government policies should change.

In Singapore, we have several political parties, though one party has led the government for a very long time. Mr Jeyaretnam's passing is a reminder that many different people, even from smaller parties, work hard because they care deeply about our country's future.

lenses.me: This might be the first time you've heard about a political party other than the ruling one — it's a good chance to learn how Singapore's democracy works.
lenses.family: Your parents might use this as a chance to explain what political parties do and why people sometimes choose different ones to represent them.
lenses.friends: You and your classmates could talk about how many political parties Singapore actually has, beyond the one most people know.
lenses.nation: It's a reminder that Singapore's political scene includes many voices, even smaller parties, who all contribute ideas about how to run the country.
lenses.world: Many countries have multiple political parties competing for votes — Singapore's system, though different, shares this same idea of democracy.

compo_words.meaning: very committed to a cause or work
compo_words.example: He was a dedicated politician who spoke up for his beliefs for many years.

compo_words.meaning: a political party that is not currently in power
compo_words.example: The Reform Party is one of Singapore's opposition parties.

compo_words.meaning: something a person leaves behind after they are gone
compo_words.example: His legacy includes years of speaking up for economic reform.

compo_words.meaning: able to keep going despite difficulties
compo_words.example: He remained resilient even while facing serious illness.

compo_phrases.example: He always stood up for what he believed was right for Singapore.
The leader left behind a legacy of dedication to his cause.

questions: Do you know what a political party is? Can you name one in Singapore?
Why do you think it's important for a country to have more than one political party?
How would you feel if you learnt that someone who worked hard for their beliefs had passed away?

From what I understand...
This makes me realise that...
I feel that...

sources.name: CNA - Reform Party leader Kenneth Jeyaretnam dies aged 67

stories.title: Singaporean Caught in Illegal Vape Factory Raid in Jakarta
stories.headline_kid: Police in Jakarta arrested a Singaporean man after finding a secret factory making thousands of dangerous vape cartridges.
summary: Police in North Jakarta, Indonesia, raided a house and discovered a hidden factory making vape cartridges filled with a substance called etomidate. They seized thousands of cartridges along with laboratory equipment used to make them, and arrested several people — including one Singaporean.

Etomidate is normally a strong medicine doctors use to make patients sleepy before surgery. Putting it into vapes is illegal and very dangerous, because it can seriously harm the brain and body when misused. In Singapore, vapes containing etomidate are sometimes called 'kpods' and have caused serious health scares among young people.

This arrest shows how illegal vape-making operations don't just stay in one country — they can cross borders, with ingredients, equipment and even people moving between places like Indonesia and Singapore.

Singapore already has strict laws banning vapes completely. This case is a strong reminder of why the government keeps warning students and teens to stay far away from vaping, no matter how 'cool' it might seem to some people.

lenses.me: It's a good reminder that vaping, especially kpods, isn't just against school rules — it can involve real, dangerous drugs.
lenses.family: Your parents might use this news to talk to you about the real dangers behind vaping, not just the rules against it.
lenses.friends: If a classmate ever talks about vaping, you'll now know it could involve harmful substances like etomidate.
lenses.nation: Singapore works closely with neighbouring countries like Indonesia to track down and stop illegal vape and drug networks.
lenses.world: It shows how criminal groups can operate across country borders to make and sell harmful products to unsuspecting buyers.

compo_words.meaning: against the law
compo_words.example: Making vapes with etomidate is illegal in both Singapore and Indonesia.

compo_words.meaning: strong action taken to stop illegal activity
compo_words.example: The police launched a crackdown on illegal vape factories.

compo_words.meaning: a group of connected people or places working together
compo_words.example: The criminal network moved vape products across borders.

compo_words.meaning: to take something by force, usually by the police
compo_words.example: Officers managed to seize thousands of illegal vape cartridges.

compo_phrases.meaning: take strong action to stop something illegal
compo_phrases.example: Authorities are cracking down on illegal vape sales.
Selling drug-laced vapes puts the lives of young users at risk.

questions: Why do you think vaping is completely banned for people in Singapore?
What would you say to a friend who wanted to try vaping because 'everyone else is doing it'?
How do you think countries can work together to stop illegal factories like this one?
phrases: I believe vaping is dangerous because...
If my friend asked me to try it, I would say...

stories.title: Super Smart Phones That Can Think and Act Like Little Robots
stories.headline_kid: Chinese companies showed off phones so clever, they can plan and complete tasks for you all on their own!
summary: At the 2026 World Artificial Intelligence Conference in Shanghai, Chinese tech companies showed off phones packed with powerful AI, or artificial intelligence — computer 'brains' that can think and learn a bit like humans do.

These new phones use something called 'agentic AI'. This means the AI doesn't just answer your questions — it can actually complete tasks for you, like booking a taxi, ordering food or planning your day, almost like having a tiny helpful assistant living inside your phone.

Imagine if your phone was like a super-efficient helper who not only tells you it's going to rain, but also reminds you to bring your umbrella and even messages your mum that you'll be a little late — all without you asking twice.

This kind of technology may soon appear in phones everywhere, including in Singapore. It's exciting because it could make life easier, but it also raises questions about privacy — since these AI helpers need to know a lot about you to do their job well.

lenses.me: Future phones might do tasks for you automatically, like helping you organise your homework schedule.
lenses.family: Your parents might one day buy phones with these smart features, and your family will need to learn how to use them safely.
lenses.friends: Your friends might soon be talking about their new 'AI assistant' apps that do things for them.
lenses.nation: Singapore's own Smart Nation initiatives are also exploring similar AI tools to help make daily life more convenient.
lenses.world: It shows a global race between countries to build the smartest, most helpful technology first.

compo_words.meaning: a new idea, method or invention
compo_words.example: The agentic AI phone is a remarkable innovation in technology.

compo_words.meaning: to display something so people can see how good it is
compo_words.example: Companies gathered in Shanghai to showcase their latest inventions.

compo_words.meaning: the most modern and advanced
compo_words.example: The conference featured cutting-edge smartphones with AI brains.

compo_words.meaning: making things easier or saving effort
compo_words.example: An AI assistant that books appointments for you is very convenient.

compo_phrases.meaning: more advanced than others at the time
compo_phrases.example: These AI phones are ahead of the curve compared to older models.

compo_phrases.meaning: completely change how something is done
compo_phrases.example: This technology could revolutionise the way we use our phones daily.

stories.title: Why China's Wild Weather Could Affect Singapore Too
stories.headline_kid: Floods, storms and record heat are hitting China this summer — and scientists say our warming planet is to blame.
summary: China has been having a summer of extreme weather — heavy floods, powerful storms and rising temperatures. In fact, rescuers are currently racing to search for survivors after a landslide in Chongqing, a city in southwest China, made even more dangerous by unstable, wet weather.

Scientists explain that China is warming faster than the world's average. This is partly because land heats up quicker than the ocean, just like how the sand at the beach gets much hotter under the sun than the sea water beside it. Since China has such a huge landmass, this effect is stronger there.

Even though these events are happening far from Singapore, weather in Asia is connected — like puzzle pieces that fit together. Extreme storms and shifting weather patterns in the region can affect rainfall here, shipping routes that bring goods to our shops, and even the haze that sometimes drifts our way.

This 'summer of extremes' is being seen by experts as a warning sign of global climate change — a reminder that even weather happening in another country can eventually touch our own lives here in Singapore.

lenses.me: It's a lesson that weather happening far away can still matter to you, like affecting prices of things shipped through affected areas.
lenses.family: If your family is planning a trip to China or nearby countries, it's worth checking weather safety updates before you go.
lenses.friends: You and your classmates could discuss in class how climate change affects countries across Asia, not just one place.
lenses.nation: Singapore, being low-lying, must also prepare for more extreme weather in the future because of climate change.
lenses.world: It shows that climate change is a shared global problem — no single country can solve it alone.

compo_words.meaning: very intense or severe
compo_words.example: China experienced extreme weather with floods and storms this summer.

compo_words.meaning: a large area of land
compo_words.example: China's huge landmass warms up faster than the surrounding ocean.

compo_words.meaning: hard to know what will happen next
compo_words.example: The unpredictable weather made the rescue operation more difficult.

compo_words.meaning: causing great and sudden damage
compo_words.example: The landslide caused catastrophic damage to nearby homes.

compo_phrases.meaning: cause a lot of damage or chaos
compo_phrases.example: The heavy floods wreaked havoc across several towns.

compo_phrases.meaning: an event that makes people realise something important
compo_phrases.example: This summer's extreme weather is a wake-up call about climate change.

questions: Have you noticed any unusual weather in Singapore recently? What was it like?
Why do you think it's important for countries to work together on climate change?
What can you do, even as a kid, to help fight climate change?
phrases: I noticed that the weather has been...
I think countries should work together because...
One small thing I can do is...
This makes me feel concerned about...
In the future, I hope that...

sources.name: CNA - Summer of extremes: How a faster-warming China is reshaping weather conditions
